questions.questionText: Explain the concept of planning fallacy

studentAnswer.answerText: The planning fallacy is a cognitive bias where individuals underestimate the time, costs, and risks of future actions while overestimating the benefits. This phenomenon was first identified by Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky in 1979. The main causes include optimism bias, where people focus on best-case scenarios, and the failure to account for past experiences with similar tasks. People tend to think about the specific task at hand rather than drawing on their general knowledge of how long similar tasks have taken in the past. This inside view leads to systematic underestimation. Additionally, social and motivational factors play a role, as people may feel pressure to commit to optimistic timelines.

The main causes include optimism bias, where people focus on best-case scenarios, and the failure to account for past experiences with similar tasks.

questions.questionText: Explain psychological factors related to planning fallacy

studentAnswer.answerText: Several psychological factors contribute to the planning fallacy. First, optimism bias causes people to believe they are less likely to experience negative events than others. Second, the focusing illusion makes people concentrate on the task itself while ignoring external factors that could cause delays. Third, anchoring bias means initial estimates become reference points that are insufficiently adjusted. Fourth, the availability heuristic leads people to recall successful completions more easily than failures. Finally, motivated reasoning causes people to unconsciously favor information that supports their desired timeline.

questions.questionText: Address the following questions using your own words with proper APA citation: Psychologists normally adopt the 'three Ds' (i.e., deviance, dysfunction, and distress) to define abnormality. Which ONE of the 'Ds' do you think a better dimension to define abnormal behaviors? Illustrate your stance with a specific psychological disorder.

studentAnswer.answerText: Among the three Ds used to define abnormality—deviance, dysfunction, and distress—I think dysfunction is the most convincing dimension because it focuses on whether a behavior interferes with normal life. A behavior may look unusual or even upsetting, but the most important question is whether it prevents a person from studying, working, maintaining relationships, or taking care of themselves. In that sense, dysfunction is a practical and meaningful way to judge abnormality.One reason dysfunction is stronger than deviance is that deviance can vary across cultures and situations. A behavior that seems strange in one group may be acceptable in another. If we rely too much on deviance, we may confuse difference with disorder. Dysfunction avoids that problem because it asks about the actual impact of the behavior on daily functioning. This makes it more useful in psychology, where the goal is not just to label difference, but to understand when someone needs help.A good example is obsessive-compulsive disorder, or OCD. People with OCD often experience unwanted thoughts and feel forced to repeat certain actions such as checking, cleaning, or counting. At first glance, these behaviors may seem strange to other people. However, the real issue is that they can take up a lot of time and interfere with normal routines. For example, a person may check the door many times before leaving home, causing them to be late for work or school. Another person may spend so much time on cleaning rituals that they cannot focus on other responsibilities. In this case, the disorder is not only about unusual behavior, but about how much it disrupts everyday life.Research has shown that OCD can seriously affect quality of life and functioning. People with OCD may struggle with work, family life, and social relationships because their symptoms are difficult to manage. This supports the idea that dysfunction is a key feature of abnormality. In fact, even if a person does not openly express much distress, the disorder may still be obvious because it disrupts their ability to function normally. That is why dysfunction can be more informative than distress alone.Distress is still important, of course, because many disorders involve suffering, anxiety, sadness, or fear. But distress is not always the clearest sign. Some people may hide their suffering, and others may feel distress for reasons that are temporary or not related to mental disorder. Dysfunction is easier to observe because it shows up in real-life problems such as missed deadlines, poor performance, withdrawal from others, or inability to complete normal tasks. This makes it a more balanced and practical criterion.In conclusion, dysfunction is the best of the three Ds because it shows whether a behavior is actually harming a person’s life. Deviance may be too culturally dependent, and distress may not always be obvious, but dysfunction clearly shows when behavior is interfering with daily functioning. OCD is a useful example because its symptoms may look odd, but what makes the disorder significant is the way it disrupts normal living. For these reasons, dysfunction is a strong dimension for defining abnormal behavior.

questions.questionText: Address the following questions using your own words with proper APA citation: Psychologists normally adopt the 'three Ds' (i.e., deviance, dysfunction, and distress) to define abnormality. Which ONE of the 'Ds' do you think a better dimension to define abnormal behaviors? Illustrate your stance with a specific psychological disorder.

studentAnswer.answerText: Among the three Ds used to define abnormality—deviance, dysfunction, and distress—I think dysfunction is the strongest dimension because it focuses on whether a behavior actually interferes with a person’s ability to live normally. A behavior may be unusual, emotionally painful, or different from social expectations, but what matters most clinically is whether it disrupts daily functioning. This makes dysfunction a practical and balanced way to judge abnormality.One major advantage of dysfunction is that it avoids the problem of cultural bias. Deviance can be misleading because what is considered “normal” varies across cultures, communities, and historical periods. A behavior that seems strange in one context may be completely acceptable in another. Distress is also useful, but it is not always enough on its own, because some people may suffer without having a mental disorder, while others may not clearly express their suffering even when their functioning is badly affected. Dysfunction gets closer to the real question psychologists should ask: is the person able to study, work, maintain relationships, and care for themselves effectively?A strong example is obsessive-compulsive disorder, or OCD. People with OCD may have intrusive thoughts and feel driven to perform repeated rituals such as checking locks, washing hands, or arranging objects in a very specific way. These behaviors may look odd to outsiders, but the real issue is the degree to which they interfere with everyday life. For instance, a student with OCD might spend so much time checking homework or re-reading notes that they cannot finish assignments on time. A worker might repeatedly return home to make sure appliances are turned off, causing lateness and stress. In such cases, the behavior is abnormal not because it is merely unusual, but because it disrupts the person’s daily functioning.OCD also shows why dysfunction is more convincing than deviance alone. Some compulsive behaviors may appear strange, but strange behavior does not automatically mean mental disorder. What makes OCD clinically significant is that the obsessions and compulsions consume time, create barriers to normal routine, and reduce quality of life. This is why many psychological definitions of disorder emphasize impairment or disability. The focus is not simply on difference, but on the extent to which the behavior prevents a person from living well.Dysfunction is also useful because it is easier to observe in practical terms. Psychologists can look at whether a person is missing school, losing jobs, avoiding responsibilities, or struggling with relationships. These signs give a clearer picture than relying only on whether someone feels upset or behaves differently. In OCD, the impairment often becomes visible through poor concentration, exhaustion, lateness, social withdrawal, and constant interference with normal tasks. That makes dysfunction a strong indicator that intervention may be needed.In conclusion, dysfunction is the best of the three Ds because it captures the real-life impact of abnormal behavior. Deviance may be too dependent on social norms, and distress may not always be clear or sufficient, but dysfunction shows whether the behavior is truly harming a person’s ability to function. OCD is a useful example because its symptoms become most meaningful when they interfere with ordinary living. For that reason, dysfunction is the most effective dimension for defining abnormal behavior.

studentAnswer.answerText: Among the three Ds used to define abnormality—deviance, dysfunction, and distress—I believe dysfunction is the best dimension because it focuses on whether a behavior actually interferes with a person’s ability to live normally. A behavior may be unusual or even upsetting, but if it does not disrupt daily life, it may not truly be abnormal in a clinical sense.Dysfunction is the strongest criterion because mental disorders are not only about differences from social norms. Many people behave in ways that are uncommon, yet they still function well at school, at work, and in relationships. What makes behavior clinically concerning is when it prevents a person from carrying out ordinary responsibilities. For this reason, dysfunction is more practical than deviance, which can be strongly influenced by culture and social expectations. It is also more useful than distress alone, because some people may suffer emotionally without having a mental disorder, while others may not fully recognize their own suffering even when their functioning is seriously impaired.A good example is obsessive-compulsive disorder. People with OCD often have persistent intrusive thoughts and repeated rituals such as checking, cleaning, or counting. At first glance, these behaviors may seem merely strange or excessive. However, the real problem is that they consume time and interfere with normal functioning. A student may spend so long repeating rituals that they cannot finish assignments. A person may repeatedly check the stove or door and become late for work. In severe cases, OCD can damage family relationships, social life, and overall quality of life. Research has shown that OCD is associated with major functional impairment and reduced quality of life.This example shows why dysfunction should be prioritized. The behavior is not abnormal simply because it looks odd to others. It is abnormal because it disrupts the person’s ability to function effectively in daily life. This makes dysfunction a more objective and clinically meaningful standard than deviance. A behavior that is unusual in one setting may be perfectly acceptable in another. Dysfunction avoids this problem by asking whether the person’s life is actually being harmed.Dysfunction also has an important advantage in treatment. If a psychologist identifies that a behavior is impairing functioning, they can focus on helping the person regain control, independence, and stability. In OCD, treatment often aims to reduce compulsive rituals so that the person can return to a more normal routine. This shows that dysfunction is not only a way to define abnormality, but also a way to understand the real need for intervention. Mental disorder definitions in the DSM tradition also emphasize clinically significant impairment, which supports the importance of dysfunction.In conclusion, dysfunction is the best of the three Ds because it identifies behavior that truly interferes with everyday living. While deviance and distress are useful, they are less reliable as single criteria. questions.questionText: Address the following questions using your own words with proper APA citation: Psychologists normally adopt the 'three Ds' (i.e., deviance, dysfunction, and distress) to define abnormality. Which ONE of the 'Ds' do you think a better dimension to define abnormal behaviors? Illustrate your stance with a specific psychological disorder.

studentAnswer.answerText: Abnormality is assessed through the three dimensions, including distress, dysfunction, deviance. Among the three, distress is a better dimension for defining abnormality because it directly addresses the emotional suffering. Psychological disorders often involve by significant emotional pain with clinically significant distress as a key diagnostic criteria and indicator (APA, 2013). By focusing on the individual’s subjective experience, distress aligns with the of person-centered psychology emphasis on alleviating emotional suffering and incongruence (Sanders & Joseph, 2016). By examining distress in the context of Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), this essay demonstrates its role as a critical, measurable, and clinically significant dimension of abnormality. Distress is a good dimension in reflecting the individual's subjective suffering and motivates help-seeking. In DSM-5, MDD is characterized by persistent depressed mood, loss of interest or pleasure in activities, and other symptoms that cause significant distress to the individual (APA,2013). In particular, the depressed mood and distress manifests as feelings of sadness, emptiness, hopelessness, accompanied by cognitive disturbance such as low self-esteem, self-depreciation, worthlessness and suicidal thoughts (Stefanis & Stefanis,1999). Patients may suffer from recurrent thoughts of death and suicidal ideation, causing immense distress to their well-being. These maladaptive distress captures the internal emotional pain or discomfort experienced by the person, which contributes to the primary reason and motive to seek psychological help or treatment (Kearney & Trull,2018). Higher self-reported distress can significantly predicted elevations of depression (Pfund et al.,2017), showcasing its role as a defining abnormality.Although distress is subjective, the severity of suffering can be measured different existing measures, such as Kessler Psychological Distress Scale (Kessler,1996) and Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) (Indiana State Medical Association,2024), which quantifies distress by assessing symptoms such as guilt, tearfulness, and suicidal ideation through patient self-reporting. The severity of depressive symtoms become more quantifiable and assists clinicians on evaluating the patient’s well-being. Recognizing that the subjective experience and expression of distress are not uniform across all individuals and cultures, cultural concepts of distress are also addressed in the DSM-5. The framework includes cultural syndromes and the local interpretation of distress, for instance Nervios and Susto include emotional distress symptoms in Latin America, resemble symptoms in MDD. (American Psychiatric Association,2013). Locally recognized syndromes and culturally specific ways of expressing suffering are addressed. Solving the concern of misinterpreting culturally normative behaviors. Clinicians can better understand and validate the patient's distress within their specific cultural context, leading to more culturally sensitive and effective treatment plans. Distress with cultural context consideration makes distress a better dimension than deviance. Deviance from statistics and social norms is a highly quantifiable and measurable indicator of abnormality. However, it is highly context-specific; what is deviant in one culture may be acceptable in another (Kearney & Trull,2018). This limitation makes deviance a less comprehensive indicator of abnormality, particularly in the case of MDD, which is not included as a diagnostic criterion.  Distress also precedes and often leads to impairments in normal functioning. Dysfunction focuses on the practical consequences of psychological symptoms, in particular when patients failed to fulfil their independent, social and occupational function (Kearney & Trull,2018). While dysfunction is essential for defining abnormality, it is based on identified behaviours that stem from distress. It may not fully capture the internal experience of individuals who maintain external functionality despite significant psychological challenges. In major depressive disorders, patients first suffer from depressed mood and losing interest or pleasure in activities they used to enjoy, which is categorized as anhedonia (Stefanis & Stefanis,1999). The diminished motivation contributes to feelings of frustration, sadness, and hopelessness. Consequently, this distress impairs pleasure received from social interactions over time, negatively affecting their motivation to perform at school or at work. Patients begin to withdraw from social activities as these interactions no longer provide emotional reward or satisfaction. Thus, dysfunction in social and occupational functioning are secondary consequences that emerge after distress is experienced. Abnormality should be defined with distress as long as the patient faces internal struggles, before it exacerbates and manifests in daily function impairments. When a patient reports significant and persistent distress that impacts their quality of life, it signals a genuine need for attention and support and abnormality, regardless of whether their behavior is overtly deviant or their functional impairment is readily apparent. In conclusion, while deviance and dysfunction offer valuable perspectives on abnormal behavior, distress stands out as a particularly salient and ethically important dimension. Its focus on the individual's internal experience of suffering provides a more direct and person-centered understanding of psychological problems. By prioritizing distress, alongside careful consideration of deviance and dysfunction, a more comprehensive and compassionate understanding in defining abnormal behavior can be achieved.
